summary: Gruber examines Bruce Tog Tognazzini's famous claim that the mouse is faster than the keyboard, based on Apple's late-80s user testing. The research found that while users perceive keyboard shortcuts as faster, stopwatch measurements show mousing is actually quicker because users experience a hidden cognitive delay recalling which keys to press. Gruber accepts the core finding but argues Tog's argument has important exceptions: two-handed editing operations and, most significantly, repetitive actions. With repetitive tasks, the recall penalty is paid only once, making keyboard shortcuts a clear winner. He concludes with a practical design guideline: commands that are likely to be invoked several times in quick succession deserve keyboard shortcuts, even if they're used infrequently overall.
key_points: Apple's user testing showed mousing is measurably faster than keyboarding, but users perceive the opposite due to cognitive amnesia during shortcut recall
Keyboard shortcuts win for repetitive actions because the recall penalty is only paid once, then amortized across multiple invocations
Two-handed input (selecting with mouse, acting with keyboard) is another genuine exception where shortcuts outperform mousing
Commands that tend to be invoked several times in short order deserve keyboard shortcuts, even if used infrequently overall
The 20-year-old research likely still holds, and mousing times may have actually improved as mouse familiarity has become universal
money_quotes: Test subjects consistently report that keyboarding is faster than mousing. The stopwatch consistently proves mousing is faster than keyboarding.
It takes two seconds to decide upon which special-function key to press. Deciding among abstract symbols is a high-level cognitive function.
The time-slice spent making the decision simply ceases to exist.

While the keyboard users have been engaged in a process so fascinating that they have experienced amnesia, the mouse users have been so disengaged that they have been able to continue thinking about the task they are trying to accomplish.
This nugget has been controversial ever since Tog first published it in 1989, mainly, I think, because it's just so hard to accept that our perception could be so wrong.
The key to understanding Tog's argument about why the mouse is faster is that there's a hidden delay where you stop to recall the actual keys to press for a shortcut.
Even if you waste two seconds remembering that the shortcut for Find Next is Command-G, you save time (versus using the mouse) if you invoke it several times in a row.

It's obvious that commands that are used frequently should get shortcuts, but so too should commands that, even if they're used infrequently, are likely to be invoked several times in short order when they are used.
key_insight: Keyboard shortcuts beat the mouse for repetitive actions because the cognitive cost of recalling the shortcut is paid only once, making them a clear efficiency win whenever a command is invoked several times in quick succession.
